title: 'Not the Australian way': PM announces new plan to tackle fuel crisis, calls out fuel hoarders
text:
Prime Minister ﻿ A﻿nthony Albanese has announced that the government will be granted new powers to help private importers bring more fuel into the nation as the government attempts to curb spiking fuel prices and panic buying.

The new powers are set to come into effect on Monday and will allow the government to use public funds to underwrite the purchase of fuel by the private sector.﻿

The prime minister said it will also allow for the purchase of fertiliser and other essentials.

The PM said the new powers are set to come into effect on Monday. (Nine)

"I want us to have the strongest possible plants, so we're ready for what may come. I want to keep our people, our economy and our nation moving," he said.

Albanese said that the government is in talks with local and international fuel suppliers "﻿to help firm up and source new cargoes required to keep Australia moving".

"Put simply, we will use Export Finance Australia to underwrite the purchase of shiploads of fuel that will add to supply here in Australia.

"This support from the government will not be business as usual.

"﻿It has to be additional supplies that are available on the international market, and it literally will be underwriting the purchase of shiploads of fuel to get here to Australia."

The prime minister said the measures are being enacted to keep Australia prepared and ensure fuel "gets to the right places".

He said that recent footage circulating on social media showing people filling multiple jerry cans at pet﻿rol stations to hoard fuel is "not the Australian way".

"People need to take what they need and no more," he said.

"I don't want things to be mandated, common sense should apply as well."

"This isn't toilet paper that's been piled up in some garages. It's actually fuel.

"That's not sensible on a range of levels. One, it's not doing the right thing by your fellow Australians. But importantly, as well, it can be dangerous."

Climate Change and Energy Minister Chris Bowen. (Supplied)

Independent petrol stations in regional Queensland were being affected by supply issues, Goondiwindi Mayor Lawrence Springborg said. (9News)

Minister for Climate Change and Energy Chris Bowen backed the prime minister's sentiments, ﻿saying that fuel demand in rural Australia is at "a very busy time".

"[Demand] remains exceedingly high, and this supply is still not enough, but we are increasing supply and working in a very complicated supply chain to get the fuel to where it needs to go.﻿"

"The fact of the matter is, cargo internationally available, but is getting increasingly expensive and increasingly risky in a volatile environment."﻿

Bowen said Australia currently has 1.6 billion litres of petrol, which roughly equates to ﻿nine days' worth, which is up "very slightly".

He also said the nation has 2.7 billion litres of diesel, which is approximately one month's worth, and is "pretty flat".﻿

"What that tells me is that while the fuel is flowing strongly out the door, especially to regional Australia."﻿

Bowen said that Australians need to remain prepared as the war in the Middle East escalates, so too will volatile fuel prices﻿.

title: Queensland could fast-track the opening of an oil lake the size of Singapore
text:
As the fuel crisis continues to deepen, the Queensland government is reportedly looking to fast-track the opening of an oil lake the size of Singapore .

The Queensland government has already granted several companies approval to conduct exploration work at the Taroom Trough, an oil lake﻿ the size of Singapore, just 300km west of Brisbane .

The investigation of the site will allow the government to learn by the end of 2026 ﻿whether it is viable to drill into the lake and extract the supplies.

The Taroom Trough is just 300km west of Brisbane. (Nine/Today)

While it is not an immediate relief from the ongoing shortage, if it is deemed usable, oil could be extracted from the site as early as 2028.﻿

In Queensland alone, more than 100 service stations were out of fuel yesterday﻿, with even more shortages across the nation.

The reports come ﻿just days out from the federal government being poised to reveal a national emergency fuel plan that will allow industries that require the supplies most to secure quantities.

An Ampol petrol station in Neutral Bay, a favourite for motorists on Sydney's Northern Beaches, has been run dry. (Supplied)

The plan would aim to create safeguards for supplies going to industries, such as farming, trucking and emergency services, as well as to regional areas.

There are calls from the opposition, however, to slash the fuel excise ﻿to immediately reduce costs for drivers.

"When you fill up your car, about 52 cents every single litre goes to taxation," Shadow Foreign Minister Ted O'Brien told Today .

"So we need to ﻿cut that taxation in half for a three-month period."

Cutting the tax would carry consequences for the federal budget, which brings in billions of dollars each year.﻿

However, the fuel crisis is expected to affect the budget regardless.﻿

title: Apprentices like Tom are considering 'packing it in' as fuel crisis bites
text:
Exclusive: Australia is in the grips of a tradie shortage and the fuel crisis could make it worse as apprentices struggling to make ends meet consider leaving trades.

Average apprentice pay rates vary depending on the trade and an apprentice's age and ﻿years on the job, and most have been stung by fuel price hikes .

Tom Brooks, a 20-year-old second year plumbing apprentice from rural WA, makes about $650 a week and is already considering other avenues to make money.

Tom Brooks, a 20-year-old second year plumbing apprentice from rural WA. (Supplied)

About a third of his weekly wage goes to fuel and he expects that figure will rise in the weeks ahead.

﻿"I'm traveling about 70km every day for work, so it adds up quick," he told nine.com.au.

"It's made budgeting for other things like food a lot harder."

Have you got a story? Contact reporter Maddison Leach at mleach@nine.com.au

The servo closest to his home currently has diesel at $3.15 a litre.

If he drives a little further afield to the town where he works it's $3 a litre.

Brooks expects those prices to keep rising, even with local diesel limits in place.

Most servos in his area have a limit of 40 litres per person, but it can be as low as 20 litres per person at locations where supplies are running out.

He hasn't been able to get a full tank in weeks.﻿

"It's been a bit of a 'buy where you can, when you can' thing," he said.

As the ﻿ conflict in the Middle East ﻿drags on, the added financial strain could push some apprentices out of trades altogether.

Nearly 60 per cent of plumbing apprentices need help with the cost of getting to work or TAFE, according to a Rheem Australia survey conducted before the fuel crisis hit.

Three in five also struggle to cover living expenses such as rent, ﻿groceries and bills.

Those figures are likely higher now that petrol has surpassed $2.50 a litre and diesel is over $3 a litre in parts of Australia.

"Everyone this crisis is probably thinking, 'I can't afford to be an apprentice anymore, so I'm just going to have to find another job to pay the bills,'" Brooks said.

"A lot of apprentices, I think, will just pack it in."

That could spell disaster for Australia's construction industry.

The ﻿construction industry is facing a critical shortage of workers, especially carpenters, electricians and plumbers.

That's concerning when the federal government has set a target to build 1.2 million homes over five years under the National Housing Accord.

The ﻿Housing Industry Association (HIA) said Australia needed more tradespeople for there to be any hope of hitting that ambitious construction target.

But the current shortage may actually worsen if the fuel crisis drags on much longer.

There is support available to keep struggling apprentices in trades.﻿

Apprentices working in clean energy or housing construction are eligible for the Key Apprenticeship Program (KAP) Apprentice Incentive, which pays up to $10,000 for full-time apprentices or $5000 for part-timers.

Those training in a priority occupation may be eligible for the ﻿Australian Apprentice Training Support Payment (AATSP) of up to $2500.

The payments are spread out in instalments across ﻿three to four years.

Apprentices can also apply for Australian Apprenticeship Support Loans﻿ of up to $25,983, paid in monthly instalments.

There are also industry-specific options such as the ﻿Rheem Apprentice Plumber Grant, which Brooks received.

Even with financial support, some apprentices still struggle to make ends meet when the price of rent, groceries, bills and now fuel just keeps rising﻿.

"I think all apprentices really feel a shitload of stress [about] cost of living at the moment," Brooks said.

"At some stage it just becomes impossible to be an apprentice and afford to live."

Some will push through with financial support from friends and family, while others – Brooks included – look for side hustles to supplement their wages.

Others will leave trades altogether to pursue higher-paying jobs.﻿

title: Iconic outback music festival Big Red Bash cancelled due to flooding
text:
Q﻿ueensland 's iconic Big Red Bash music festival has been cancelled due to widespread outback flooding .

Organisers had been attempting to relocate the festival from its Birdsville location after intense rainfall flooded the region.﻿

"After careful consideration of the available options, we decided that relocating the Birdsville Big Red Bash was not feasible for 2026," event founder and managing director Greg Donovan said.

READ MORE: Fines for dodgy petrol pricing doubled as fuel crisis continues

The Big Red Bash, the self-proclaimed world's most remote music ﻿festival, is scrambling to find another venue after a rare deluge hit outback Queensland. (Facebook)

"Mother Nature is the highest authority; this year she has spoken and we have heard the message."

He said patrons were surveyed before the decision was made to cancel.

The event was 90 per cent sold out.

The 2026 line-up included Missy Higgins, Hoodoo Gurus, The Teskey Brothers, The Living End, Birds of Tokyo, Jessica Mauboy, Shannon Noll, Kate Ceberano, The Whitlams, Tim Finn, among others.

Organisers say next year's event will go ahead with ﻿the majority of this year's line-up confirmed for 2027.

The three-day event has drawn thousands of campers to the edge of the Simpson Desert for more than 10 years.

Thousands kick up the dust to attempt Nutbush dance world record View Gallery

"We fully understand and appreciate the planning, excitement and anticipation all our adventurous patrons have when they buy tickets and lock in plans to head into the great Australian outback for the Bash," Donovan said.

"We also know our own team's excitement and passion for delivering the festival in this unique and amazing location. It's a very special and spiritual place."

Ticketholders can request a refund via Oztix, ﻿transfer their tickets to the 2027 Birdsville Big Red Bash or transfer to the 2026 Broken Hill Mundi Mundi Bash from 20–22 August 2026.

title: 'All the villages will be deserted’: The crisis emptying Greek islands and towns
text:
Greece is spending billions to reverse an alarming population loss that some are calling an existential threat.

The afternoon sun has cast the hills of Arcadia in a pale gold, but the village of Lasta is in cold shadow.

Many of its stone buildings lie in ruins.

There were once around 300 people living here in Lasta, a village in the rugged mountains of Greece's Peloponnese region – now there are just three.

"The villages, they are becoming ghost towns," says Lambros Papalambros, a walking-tour guide and lifelong resident of the area, as we climb the steep path into town.

The village of Lasta in Arcadia, Greece, has become a 'ghost town' after almost complete depopulation. ( )

Swings outside the closed school in Lasta, Greece. ( )

The bell now tolls for none. ( )

An empty square behind the church in Lasta. ( )

In recent years, this deserted village has become something of a tourism novelty, not least for its self-service café.

There are no staff, so visitors make their own coffee, clean up after themselves and leave a donation.

But for Lambros, there is a deep sense of loss here, too.

Inside the café, the walls are adorned with fading snapshots of the proud town he remembers visiting as a boy.

"There were farmers, a lot of children," he recalls. "This place was full of life."

Lasta is by no means alone in suffering dire population loss.

By some estimates, there are over a hundred depopulated or abandoned villages across the country.

"It's a problem all around where we are at the moment," says Lambros. "And it's a shame."

Tour guide Lambros Papalambros takes walking groups to the 'ghost town' of Lasta. ( )

Fading photos and mementos from the town's past adorn the walls of the cafe in Lasta. ( )

It is also a glimpse into Greece's possible future.

A plunging birth rate, mass emigration and cost-of-living pressures are putting the country at risk of population collapse.

By some predictions, Greece's population could drop by as much as 20 per cent by 2050, from around 10 million now, to around 8 million.

By then, the OECD projects, Greece will be Europe's oldest nation.

It is an issue the Greek Prime Minister Kyriakos Mitsotakis has called a "ticking time bomb" and a "national threat".

"It's an existential problem," says demographer Alexandra Tragaki, from Harokopio University of Athens. "This is going to cause real problems in the labour market, in the pensions system, in medical care."

A crisis and a mass exodus

The reasons for Greece's demographic decline are varied.

Like many developed countries, Greece's birth rate has been falling for decades, and now sits at just 1.3 children per woman, well below the "replacement rate" of 2.1 demographers say is necessary for a stable population.

In recent years, the country has seen nearly twice as many deaths as births.

But Greece's predicament has also been turbo-charged by its recent history, not least the years of political and economic turmoil following the 2009 debt crisis.

As youth unemployment hit nearly 60 per cent, it is estimated half-a-million mostly young Greeks fled the country for better prospects overseas, with many never returning.

Professor Tragaki says the crisis not only robbed Greece of a large cohort of reproductive-aged citizens, but it also produced a generation that is "less optimistic about the future".

"Having children means you believe in the future and you invest in the future," she says.

Klairi Sacha was among the half-a-million Greeks who fled the country during the debt crisis years. ( )

Klairi Sacha, 37, was among those who left Greece at the peak of the debt crisis, moving to London to start her career.

"It was honestly a traumatising experience," she says. "People were losing their jobs, defaulting on their mortgages."

After a few years, she tried to come home, but the country was so unstable that she made the difficult decision to leave a second time.

Klairi has since rebooted her life in Athens, but like many of her peers, her plans to start a family have had to take a back seat amid all the disruption.

"With what we went through during our twenties, we really want to make sure we have a very stable life situation before starting a family," she says.

"Even now, people who are highly educated and have a good salary, they still struggle to make ends meet. Having a family has become more difficult."

Over half the population of Greece now live in and around the crammed and often grid-locked capital.

But it is a different story in regional Greece, where some places are now seeing an acute shortage of children.

Greek families are having fewer children, a problem made worse by the debt crisis of the 2010s. ( )

Arcadia, in the north of Greece's Peloponnese region, has seen significant population loss in the past few decades. ( )

The last child in the village

In the hills of Arcadia, not far from Lasta, farmers Nikki and Jon have watched the slow demise of their village over the past decade.

Limni's bank, police station and dentist have all closed.

Gone, too, are the children. The couple's eight-year-old son, George, is the last child left in the village.

"The old people are dying, the young people don't marry, and they leave," she says. "If they continue to leave, all the villages will be deserted."

Watch Foreign Correspondent's journey through Greece now on ABC iview and YouTube.

Nikki and John hope George will one day farm cattle and goats in the hills above Limni, as they now do.

"Our life here is beautiful," says Nikki. "George likes our work very much. He loves the animals."

But there is not much left to keep him here.

Limni does not have a school anymore, so each day George travels to a larger town 20 minutes away.

There are so few kids in the area that the government does not send a school bus anymore — it sends a taxi.

Parents Nikki and John hope their son George will continue farming the hills behind their village when he grows up. ( )

Over 700 schools were closed in Greece last year because there were not enough kids to fill classrooms. ( )

Morning mist over a village in the hills of Arcadia. ( )

Across Greece, school enrolments are in freefall.

Last year, the Greek government announced the closure of more than 700 schools because there were too few kids to fill the classrooms.

That's around 5 per cent of all schools in the country in a single year.

The story made global headlines last year when Elon Musk tweeted about it, labelling it "the death of Greece".

It struck a nerve at home, too.

"It's a very sad image," says Domna Michailidou, Greece's minister of social cohesion and family affairs. "To see an historic school in the middle of a village closing down is an image that kicks a special spot in the hearts and in the souls of all of us."

Michailidou, a former economist, is among those leading efforts to reverse the country's demographic demise.

The Mitsotakis government plans to spend billions of euros over the next decade trying to reverse the birth trend and mitigate the consequences of its aging population.

This includes cash benefits and tax breaks for new parents, with a one-off payment starting at 2,400 Euros for a first child and increasing to 3,500 Euros for a fourth child.

But as a pragmatic economist, Michailidou knows that none of this is a guaranteed fix.

Tourists snap photos of the ancient Erechtheion on the Acropolis in Athens. ( )

Domna Michailidou says countries like Greece are facing a cultural shift, as young people opt out of parenthood. ( )

"If we had some kind of magic remedy in order to completely transpose this problem and inverse it, of course, we would have done it," she says.

"If people responded directly to financial incentives with regard to the demographic issue, it would've been simpler for all of us."

She says governments around the world are reckoning with a "cultural" shift towards parenting.

"Young people don't want to have kids," she says. "They defer the decision. They would rather go and travel.

"To say very simply, we also need to make it cool for the youngsters to have kids."

Watch Vanishing Greece on ABC iview A silent crisis is threatening the country's future.

But Professor Tragaki believes there is more to be done, including making childcare more affordable and accessible.

"Instead of focusing only on increasing births, we should also focus on increasing productivity, extending healthy working lives … and making better use of the human capital we already have," she says.

"The solution is not necessarily more people but better organisation of the people we have."

While immigration might seem like one obvious solution for boosting the numbers, that is not how Greece's hardline immigration minister, Thanos Plevris, sees it.

Government policy has been hardening towards immigration, following a decade in which over a million refugees arrived on Greece’s doorstep.

"I am one of those who believe that demography should not be combined with immigration," says Plevris.

"Greeks are not being born. The demographic problem will be solved if the incentives are given to give birth to more Greeks."

Greece is on track to having Europe's oldest population by 2050, according to the OECD. ( )

The Greek island of Lipsi in the Dodecanese archipelago. ( )

A baby bust in the Greek islands

While the government is rolling out a 10-year action plan, some everyday Greeks are already taking matters into their own hands.

One charity is trying to boost the birth rate by offering free maternity services for pregnant mums, especially in Greece's remote islands.

Founded in 2015 by Athens-based obstetrician-gynaecologist Stefanos Chandakas, Hope Genesis helps women who want to have children but face difficulties, either due to the lack of medical services where they live, economic hardship or fertility issues.

It helps by covering the costs of pregnancy, including paying for women to visit hospitals in Athens for medical check-ups and to give birth.

More than 700 babies have been born through the program — hardly enough to shift the dial nationally — but in some small communities, it is making a difference.

"This work that Hope Genesis does is a godsend," says Fotis Mangos, the mayor of Lispi, a tourist island in the Dodecanese archipelago with around 800 permanent residents.

Over the past 15 years, Lipsi has seen twice as many deaths as births.

"Everyone understands this is a bad development for our island," says Fotis.

Lipsi mayor Fotis Mangos is pushing for more government financial support for new parents in remote parts of Greece. ( )

The narrow streets of Lipsi. ( )

Lipsi in bloom. ( )

Sunset over the harbour in Lipsi. ( )

"It is very important for a small place to have young children, to have schools that are functioning and to have the next generation to give impetus to the development of the place."

He says a pregnancy in the Greek islands can cost four times as much, once travel for medical care is factored in.

He is pushing for the Greek government to double its financial support for new parents in places like Lipsi.

"I see that this economic insecurity does not give young couples the confidence to get married and have a family and children," he says.

In the meantime, Hope Genesis has sponsored 18 births on the island.

Young mum Despoina, who gave birth to twins Nikoletta and Dimitris through the program, says many families opt to leave when it comes time to have kids.

"People don't have children [on the island] because there are difficulties," she says, pointing to a lack of medical services, especially in an emergency. "They go to Athens, to Rhodes, to other islands, bigger ones."

Young Lipsi mum Despoina gave birth to twins on the island through the Hope Genesis program. ( )

She credits Hope Genesis with helping her stay on Lipsi throughout her pregnancy and now hopes her children will be able to raise their own families on the island.

"But what I see is that the population of the island will [mostly be] tourists," she says. "That is, it will slowly begin to be deserted by the permanent residents, the locals."

In some depopulated regions, there are now generous incentives on offer to lure new residents.

Some towns have offered free housing and jobs for young families willing to relocate.

The Greek government is also trying to spur a regional revival, paying 10,000 Euros to those who settle in areas suffering long-term population loss.

Bringing expats back to Greece

Others are looking outside of Greece to replenish the population, such as luring back those who fled during the debt crisis.

Athens-based orthodontist Eirini Kapogianni runs a group called Back to Greece, a social and professional network encouraging Greek expats to come home.

She started the group on Instagram after her own experience of returning from Germany in 2019.

"There are many Greeks who live abroad and have lived a secure life there, and it's very difficult to leave a stable career and come back to start your life from scratch," she says.

What started as an Instagram page for Greeks to share their stories about returning home has grown into a networking platform with more than 20,000 followers, which helps connect returnees with employers and like-minded friends.

Eirini Kapogianni started the social media group Back to Greece, which now has 20,000 followers. ( )

The Acropolis viewed from Lycabettus Hill. ( )

"If Greece manages to create the right conditions that will attract all these young people, makes sure that it creates the right infrastructure to welcome these young people, to offer prospects and meritocracy, then the young people will return," Eirini says, "And it can be a new era for our country."

The Greek government is also keen to reverse the brain-drain, offering tax discounts to entice young professionals home.

But it is hard to compete when Greece's wages remain below pre-crisis levels, even if the country has started to bounce back from the economic doldrums.

According to Eurostat, 46 per cent of Greeks cannot afford a one-week holiday, compared to an EU average of 27 per cent.

Klairi Sacha, who is also involved in Back to Greece, says most Greeks are ultimately drawn back by the pull of family, culture, and the magic of the place.

"Athens is a city where you have the old co-living with the new under the same sky," she says. "This is what we love about this place."

The big Greek family might be a thing of the past, says Klairi, but it remains a fundamental pillar of Greek culture.

"The Greek family is smaller now, but it's still a very powerful institution and we should do our best, in my opinion, to protect it."

Watch Foreign Correspondent's Vanishing Greece now on ABC iview and YouTube.

title: PM announces new powers to boost fuel supply amid Middle East tensions
text:
The Albanese government will use public money to underwrite the delivery of extra fuel in a bid to shore up supply during the Middle East conflict.

Hundreds of service stations across Australia are experiencing shortages of either unleaded petrol or diesel, particularly in New South Wales and Queensland.

The government urges motorists to not panic-buy. (ABC News: Ebony ten Broeke)

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has announced the government will take further measures to boost supply by underwriting the purchase of additional fuel from the international market.

Under the plan, petrol giants will still buy fuel but the government will take on the financial risk of shipments that might otherwise be too expensive.

PM leaves door open to halving fuel excise The prime minister leaves the door open to halving taxes on petrol and diesel, after the Coalition demands temporary relief from sky-high fuel prices.

"This support from the government will not be business as usual," Mr Albanese said.

"It has to be additional supplies that are available on the international market and it literally will be underwriting the purchase of shiploads of fuel to get here to Australia.

"It will give suppliers the confidence to secure additional and discretionary cargoes and can be used to service uncontracted demand, including for regional and independent fuel suppliers."

Requires legislation to be implemented

Labor will introduce legislation to parliament on Monday, seeking to amend the Export Finance and Insurance Corporation Act to grant new fuel security powers to acquire additional supply.

Tony Wood, a senior fellow in energy and climate change at the Grattan Institute, backed the move during interview on ABC's Weekend Breakfast.

Loading...

Mr Wood said he did not know how much the measures would cost taxpayers.

"How long it goes on will dictate absolutely how much money it entails," he said.

"That has some implications for the upcoming budget as well."

He said the action made sense and provided an "insurance against high costs that the companies themselves are finding hard to deal [with]" .

We want to know how much fuel costs where you are. Send us a photo. We're gathering data on fuel prices around Australia. Share a selfie of you and the fuel price board.

Panic buyers told to stop

The government has repeatedly insisted Australia's fuel shortages are being caused by panic buying — not supply constraints.

Footage circulating on social media shows people filling up multiple jerry cans with fuel at service stations.

"This isn't toilet paper that's being piled up in some garages," Mr Albanese said.

" It's actually fuel, and that's not sensible on a range of levels. "

Energy Minister Chris Bowen said Australia had more than a month's worth of fuel in reserve, including petrol and jet fuel.

While the government is taking further steps to target supply, the opposition has been focused on the cost.

Opposition Leader Angus Taylor today repeated his call for the government to halve the fuel excise — a 53 per cent flat tax per litre applied at the petrol pump.

"Our proposal will reduce the price of fuel by 26 cents a litre," he said.

Angus Taylor has again called for the government to halve the fuel excise. (ABC News: Matt Roberts)

"We are in a fuel-affordability crisis right now. They [customers] are coming to the bowser and they are shocked at what they are seeing."

Mr Albanese did not rule out cutting the fuel excise, but said his focus is on shoring up supply.

title: Remote WA area connected to outside world with first at-home internet service
text:
Thousands of kilometres from the nearest city, residents in the remote community of Ngalingkadji in Western Australia's north have been living without home internet access.

In lieu of targeted government support for residential internet services, a charity has now stepped up with ambitious goals of connecting the Australian outback to the world.

Ngalingkadji, in the Kimberley's Fitzroy Valley, is about 2,300km north of Perth. (ABC Kimberley: Andrew Seabourne)

Situated in the Kimberley's Fitzroy Valley and 2,300km north of Perth, Ngalingkadji is far from the only isolated Indigenous community facing this challenge.

The latest Mapping the Digital Gaps report shows First Nations Australians are twice as likely to be digitally excluded.

Way beyond the negative issues of social media, the positives of connection to the internet means access to online banking services, telehealth and emergency services.

'Life changing'

Local resident Gavin Smith said the Concordia Initiative's installation of free internet had made a significant difference.

"It helps with communicating with agencies and other organisations, especially in emergency situations during floods," he said.

Gavin Smith lives in the Indigenous community of Ngalingkadji, near Fitzroy Crossing. (ABC Kimberley: Ruby Littler)

Flooding and extreme weather is common in the Kimberley's wet season.

People living in Ngalingkadji have previously been left vulnerable and further isolated when they were unable to access the latest flood advice and emergency information during power outages.

Under the new system locals can stay connected even without power.

Loading...

"As soon as Concordia came in saying 'we're reaching out to the communities in regards to installing internet connection', we thought let's go for it," Mr Smith said.

" Because we don't have any sort of any communication with the outside world … it's been life-changing. "

The Concordia Initiative was founded by Peter Marchiori. (ABC Kimberley: Ruby Littler)

Concordia Initiative founder Peter Marchiori is aiming to install free internet connections in 2,030 homes across 100 remote communities by 2030.

Mr Marchiori said Ngalingkadji had been a successful pilot attempt.

"Digital inclusion is a massive issue, especially in remote communities. Our main focus is just keeping everyone connected and getting them online," he said.

A solar panel sits on a trailer in the middle of the community and connects a Starlink satellite internet service.

Ngalingkadji residents now have wi-fi access inside their homes and in public areas. (ABC Kimberley: Ruby Littler)

"That beams internet to each of the houses," Mr Marchiori said.

"Then there's a little nano station with a solar panel on each roof which keeps their internet going, whether they have power or not."

Mr Marchiori said government-funded programs only went so far and were not targeted at residential connections.

A solar panel in the middle of the community is helping power wi-fi. (ABC Kimberley: Ruby Littler)

Broader impacts

Marra Worra Worra financial advisor Marcel Sithole said internet access was key to expanding opportunities for residents.

"Access to basic essential services and being able to effectively get support is definitely a challenge faced by so many people," he said.

"It's been a really good help to see this project … for community members."

Marcel Sithole works as a financial counsellor for Aboriginal corporation Marra Worra Worra. (ABC Kimberley: Ruby Littler)

The Fitzroy Valley is devoid of bank branches, making online connectivity crucial to basic financial management, according to Mr Sithole.

He said he often saw short-term solutions and attempts at "overnight fixes" when it came to improving the lives of people in isolated communities.

"We've overcome the challenge for today," he said.

[We need to] look at the next generation and making sure that whatever the solution we're working towards will keep them in mind."

The Indigenous community of Ngalingkadji is receiving free wi-fi for the first time. (ABC Kimberley: Ruby Littler)

The federal government committed $68 million in the 2024-25 budget to close the digital gap, specifically targeting First Nations digital inclusion.

The funding included support for free community wi-fi in remote areas alongside a First Nations Digital Support Hub.

However, these programs largely focus on shared community connectivity and digital skills support, rather than household-level internet access.

title: Young Queensland runner breaks national records at state titles
text:
An 11-year-old Queensland runner has broken multiple Australian records at the Little Athletics Queensland State Championships held in Brisbane.

More than 2,000 athletes from 76 clubs statewide competed, but Sunshine Coast runner Rylan Wooster stood out.

He set Australian and Queensland records in the 1500 metres and 800m, and a Queensland record in the 400m — just days before his 11th birthday.

Rylan clocked 4 minutes 35.76 seconds in the 1500m taking 10 seconds off the previous national record.

He followed it up with 2:15.29 in the 800m taking 1.23 seconds off the previous Australian best performance.

He then won the 400m in 1:00.05, breaking the Queensland record by 1.59 seconds, completing a rare sweep across three middle-distance events.

Rylan crosses the finishing line of the 1500m at QSAC Stadium in Brisbane. ((Photo supplied: Little Athletics Queensland))

Record-breaking performance

Rylan said despite his training, he was not expecting such a record-breaking result.

"For the 1500m I definitely didn't," he said.

"But I was pretty happy."

The year 6 student only began focusing on running in recent years after success in cross country.

And he is already balancing multiple sporting dreams.

"My big dream … is to play AFL," he said.

Rebecca Wooster with her son Rylan after his record-breaking performance, says raising three sports-loving boys takes plenty of coordination and commitment. (Photo supplied: Rebecca Wooster)

Driven to succeed

His parents, Rebecca and Benjamin Wooster, have supported his rapid rise while juggling the busy sporting schedules of Rylan and his two brothers aged eight and 12.

His father said his son's determination had been clear leading into the championships.

"If he wants something, he goes out and grabs it … he'll keep trying and pushing until he gets there."

Coach Tony takes runners through drills on the Sunshine Coast. (Photo: ABC News/Janel Shorthouse)

Long-time Sunshine Coast trainer Tony Doherty, who coaches Rylan, said the young runner's mindset sets him apart.

"He's a sensational runner … very focused and he listens very well," Mr Doherty said.

"He hasn't done a lot of running, but what he does, he does quality work — and he just steps up.

"He's even running just as fast as 14, 15 and 16-year-old athletes that I coach."

On Friday nights over summer, Rylan heads along to Little Athletics at the University of the Sunshine Coast.

The club's head coach Nick Bennett said he would never forget seeing him perform on the track.

"Rylan crossed the line with a smile on his face, and he was the first to congratulate the other runners.

"He's just a very down-to-earth kid."

Mr Bennett said Little Athletics played an important role in the development of youngsters.

"[It] gives kids the opportunity to develop in a positive environment — whether they're breaking records or just breaking their personal bests," he said.

Christopher Davis, CEO of Little Athletics Queensland, says there has been a "boom" in running around the world. (Photo supplied: Little Athletics Queensland)

Running boom grows

Little Athletics Queensland chief executive Christopher Davis said it was exciting to see records tumble as participation grows.

"There's been a real running boom globally," Mr Davis said.

Gold Coast runner Ellie Eckstein also set under-14 national records in the 1500m and 800m, clocking 4:34.82 and 2:11.14. (Photo supplied: Little Athletics Queensland)

"Kids are just going out, they love running and they're getting faster and faster," he said.

With the Brisbane 2032 Olympic Games approaching, Mr Davis said that interest is expected to only increase.

Rylan encouraged other young athletes to stay committed.

"… just keep on going at it and putting in the work," he said.

"You'll get it back one day."

His mother said that mindset was already delivering results.

title: How does it feel to be stung by one of the world's most venomous jellyfish?
text:
Sarah Cipollone is used to dealing with pain.

As an osteopath living in Airlie Beach in north Queensland, she often meets and helps treat people living with complex or chronic soreness.

But nothing prepared her for the agony delivered by the sting of a fingernail-sized Irukandji jellyfish.

"It was unimaginable and indescribable pain … I really did think I was going to die," she said.

Sarah Cipollone says she wants others to be more careful in the water. (ABC Rural: Aimee Mitchell)

The highly venomous Irukandji is found in Australia's warm tropical waters between November and May.

They are almost invisible and there is a delay between contact and the searing pain, so most people will not immediately realise when they have been stung.

What does an Irukandji sting feel like?

After spending Christmas morning snorkelling, Dr Cipollone and her friends stopped their boat to flick lures at tuna before jumping "in and out" of the water to escape the stifling heat.

She did not bother putting back on the spandex stinger suit she was wearing earlier in the day.

Dr Cipollone said her back started to hurt about 15 minutes later, by the boat ramp, and she assumed it was a muscle strain.

"So, I got down on the ground to just stretch out my back and then I couldn't get back up," she said.

" My legs didn't work anymore. "

Irukandji stings can cause feelings of "impending doom" and anxiety. (ABC News: Sharnie Kim)

About 40 minutes after swimming, Dr Cipollone began to realise she had been stung when she could no longer put any pressure on her back due to the intense pain.

"And then rolling over onto my side and I'm hyperventilating and I'm panicking," she said.

She called an ambulance and was treated in Proserpine Hospital with painkillers until her symptoms began to fade hours later.

How does an Irukandji jellyfish sting work?

Marine biologist Lisa-ann Gershwin, who has studied the species for more than 20 years, said a tiny drop of venom was enough to deliver incredible pain.

"They have little, tiny, microscopic stinging cells inside, embedded in the surface of the tentacles," Dr Gershwin said.

"Each stinging cell is basically a little capsule with a harpoon coiled up inside and a hair trigger at one end.

"And inside the stinging cell and inside the harpoon, it is the venom."

Lisa-ann Gershwin has studied jellyfish for decades. (ABC TV: Compass)

When triggered by contact, the tiny "harpoon" can fly out at 40,000 times the force of gravity.

"Either you don't feel it at all, or it's really so minor that you just dismiss it," Dr Gershwin said.

She said painful symptoms generally developed between five and 45 minutes after the initial sting.

" People describe it as feeling like an electric drill is drilling into the back or like they're being hit with a cricket bat again and again. It comes in waves. "

Irukandji stings are rarely fatal with only two deaths recorded in Australia.

Dr Gershwin said scientists were still researching what chemicals or proteins caused the potentially fatal Irukandji syndrome, which is caused by some species of the jellyfish.

Symptoms include high blood pressure, abnormal heart rate, severe chest and abdominal pain, and a sense of "impending doom".

Painful box jellyfish and Irukandji are a risk to swimmers during the wet season at Eimeo Beach, near Mackay. (ABC Tropical North: Bryn Wakefield)

How is an Irukandji sting treated?

Whitsundays GP Chris Gill said treatment involved reducing pain and watching for severe symptoms including high blood pressure.

"Essentially, it's lots of pain relief," he said.

"It's hard to cut through when people are in distress, but it's always important to keep talking to them, and keep reassuring them that this is a normal response and it will settle with some pain relief."

Since September last year, at least 21 people presented to the Mackay Hospital and Health Service with symptoms.

Lifeguards conduct "stinger drags" using nets to test for jellyfish. (ABC Tropical North: Bryn Wakefield)

"If you get into distress or you need some help, there's always Triple Zero (000)," Dr Gill said.

"Just present to either the hospital or your nearest medical practitioner if you've got questions or [need] advice just call for help early."

He said dousing the affected area with vinegar was also important to neutralise the stinging cells so that they did not inject any more venom.

"Even if it's just a minor sting and you don't know what it is, just whack it with vinegar," Dr Gershwin said.

Symptoms of an Irukandji Sting

title: Ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle causes outages at major WA gas facilities
text:
Four of Australia's largest gas projects have suffered outages as ex-Tropical Cyclone Narelle continues to lash Western Australia.

Energy giants Santos, Chevron, and Woodside all confirmed impacts to critical infrastructure, although the duration of the disruption remains to be seen and the cyclone has now been downgraded to a tropical low.

Their operations account collectively for almost two-thirds of WA's domestic gas supply over the past three months, according to the Australian Energy Market Operator.

The state government said it was working to ensure a consistent domestic supply.

It comes as countries around the globe brace for a shortage of liquefied natural gas (LNG), while the conflict in the Middle East drags on.

"It's certainly bad timing from that point of view," said independent gas analyst Jeanette Roberts.

" When you look at the cost of living and fuel prices, the impact of the triple whammy could be really quite significant. "

Facilities still offline

Before it was downgraded to a tropical low early this morning, ex-tropical Cyclone Narelle carved a path through much of Australia's most productive energy infrastructure, intensifying to category four strength on Thursday.

The system captured by satellite over the WA coast on Thursday. (Supplied: Bureau of Meteorology)

Gale-force winds lashed islands off WA's Pilbara, with gusts peaking at around 159 kilometres per hour on Barrow Island — home to Chevron's flagship Gorgon gas facilities.

The company said it was still working to restore production there and at its Wheatstone project, which had an offshore platform knocked offline around midday.

"As the platform supplies feed gas to Wheatstone's onshore facilities near Onslow, LNG and domestic gas production has been suspended," a Chevron spokesperson said.

One of three LNG production trains at Chevron's Gorgon gas facility also experienced an outage around 3pm on Thursday.

Production has been suspended at Chevron's Wheatstone Gas Plant, west of Onslow in the Pilbara. (Supplied: Wheatstone)

Meanwhile, Woodside suffered a "production interruption" at its Karratha Gas Plant.

The plant is responsible for processing gas from the company's enormous North West Shelf project.

"Production at the North West Shelf Project is expected to recommence after Woodside is able to mobilise its workforce to its offshore facilities," the company said.

Woodside said it would declare any "material impact" of its Karratha gas plant disruption to the market. (ABC News: Charlie Mclean)

Santos operates a gas facility of its own at Varanus Island, 75 kilometres off the Pilbara coast.

The island bore the brunt of winds reaching 180 kilometres per hour early Thursday evening, causing the plant to trip.

"Once weather conditions have abated and personnel can safely re-enter the plant, we'll return to normal operations," a spokesperson said.

'No need to panic'

Energy and climate journalist Peter Milne described the situation as "a big deal" but cautioned against panic.

"It's going to take some time for the weather to calm down so the workers can get out there, fly offshore to the platforms, inspect them, make sure everything's okay, and fire them up again," he said.

" The unknown is how long that will take. "

Mr Milne said there would still be reserves in the Dampier-Bunbury gas pipeline as well as in underground storage.

"Gas is a pretty important fuel for our power mix in the Southwest, and that definitely will not be touched — the government will make sure of that."

Industry likely to be impacted first

Ms Roberts said the flow of LNG to WA had been interrupted before and its repercussions had been widespread.

In 2008, an explosion on Varanus Island, then owned by Apache Energy, shut down almost a third of the state's domestic supply for two months.

"That was a major issue and major damage," she said.

"There was obviously impact on users in the Pilbara, people like BHP and Rio and Burrup fertilisers

"But also 150 small businesses reported impacts as well."

Cyclone Narelle continues destructive path Severe Tropical Cyclone Narelle continues to cut a destructive swathe through Western Australia's Gascoyne even as it weakens, with authorities warning the extreme weather is not over yet.

The ABC approached multiple gas-generated power plants in WA's south for comment.

RATCH Australia, which operates the gas-fired Kemerton power station near Bunbury, said it was not anticipating any material disruptions but was "monitoring the situation carefully".

Domestic supply 'consistent'

WA Energy Minister Amber Jade Sanderson said she was not aware of the damage when asked at a press conference yesterday.

"[The plants] operate in a cyclone region, they are well prepared, and there's no disruption to domestic gas supply," she said.

WA Energy Minister Amber Jade Sanderson said there had not been a disruption to domestic gas supply thus far. (ABC News: Keane Bourke)

However, a state government spokesperson later acknowledged the disruption.

"Woodside, Santos, and Chevron have advised their offshore workforces have been successfully demobilised amid Cyclone Narelle," the spokesperson said in a statement.

"The state government continues to work with each company to ensure domestic customers are receiving a consistent supply of gas."

The outages coincide with global energy insecurity caused by the war in the Middle East.

Qatar, one of the world's largest gas exporters, was forced to suspend shipments after Iran's blockage of the Strait of Hormuz, while one of its major gas production plants came under missile fire.

title: Newlyweds sell everything to buy 160-year-old former pub
text:
Jason and Kyla Romaior were married only a few weeks when they joked about spending their life savings on buying the heritage-listed wedding venue where they had just tied the knot.

The couple expected their loved ones to tell them buying the 160-year-old wooden-slab hotel in cyclone-prone North Queensland was a stupid idea.

Instead, they were repeatedly told, "You guys should do that."

So, with the encouragement of their friends and family, Mr Romaior, 29 and Ms Romaior, 26, became the new owners of the Heritage Tea Rooms in January, just six months after their wedding.

The property, formerly the Eureka Hotel, is at Hervey Range, to the north of Townsville.

"All these different community elements had to fall in line for us to even consider doing this," Mr Romaior said.

Loading...

The 10-hectare property had sat on the market for four years before the Romaiors took on the challenge of maintaining a heritage property, 10-hour lawn mowing sessions and running a busy cafe on site.

But their decision was sealed after realising they both had a high enough risk appetite to cope with the many hurdles that could come their way.

The Eureka Hotel was built at Thornton Gap on Hervey Range in an area susceptible to tropical cyclones. (ABC News: Emily Anderson)

"Tomorrow, a storm could come, and a tree could fall on the tea rooms — we wouldn't be able to operate and we might lose all our money," Mr Romaior said.

"But that doesn't matter in the grand scheme of eternity."

Understanding the risk

The building was constructed in 1865 as the Eureka Hotel on the first inland road connecting the Port of Townsville to the new gold fields west and north of Charters Towers.

It was built out of ironbark with a corrugated iron roof and was granted state heritage status in 1992 for its association with the development of the mining and pastoral industries in North Queensland.

This photo of the Eureka Hotel at Hervey Range was taken in the 1940s. (Supplied: Heritage Tea Rooms)

"The horizontal slab structure, which is raised on low, round ironbark stumps and massive half-round timber bearers fixed with wooden pegs, is a rare example of bush carpentry," the Queensland Heritage Register said.

Throughout its history, the building changed names several times and was also used as a dance hall and refreshment room for travellers.

Its history was a key reason the Romaiors wanted the property, but also one of the reasons they believed it took four years to sell.

"It's just such a big undertaking … lots of work to run a cafe and take care of the place," Mr Romaior said.

"It's also why it's awesome, but why I think it dissuaded a lot of people."

The Romaiors have joined a long line of owners of the 1865-built Eureka Hotel at Hervey Range. (ABC News: Emily Anderson)

Though the couple was prepared for insurance costs of about $20,000 annually, they said initial expenditure had temporarily delayed some upgrades and additions.

Mr Romaior said the heritage classification also meant building maintenance could be difficult, restricted and costly.

"It's hard to know even what you can repair," he said.

"There's no ceiling, so in summer you get very hot."

The Romaiors have mitigated some costs by calling on their family and community to help.

The Eureka Hotel is heritage-listed for its significance as a rare form of bush carpentry. (ABC News: Emily Anderson)

"With my mum, we said, 'Would you consider working for us?' and that was just a yes," Ms Romaior said.

"Everything was just falling into place."

Mr Romaior said previous experience owning a business had equipped him with the management and financial skills to run the new venture.

"There's risk everywhere, but if you let that stop you, you wouldn't do anything," he said.

"Even if this all fails, we do have the kookaburras and kangaroos."

Resilient sector with lower entry barrier

Townsville business broker Susan Butler said businesses like the tea rooms were often on the market longer than conventional hospitality small businesses.

"When there's a lifestyle component, particularly in a regional or remote location, the buyer pool naturally narrows to people who genuinely want that lifestyle as well as the business," Ms Butler said.

"These opportunities don't suit everyone."

Susan Butler believes the hospitality sector is becoming more resilient despite cost-of-living pressures. (Supplied: Susan Butler)

Ms Butler, who has worked in the industry for more than 20 years, said while there were risks involved in hospitality, it was an industry with lower entry barriers than more complex businesses.

She said soaring costs were another pressure for business owners, but the pandemic and cost-of-living crises meant many had put strategies in place.

"Businesses have been forced to adapt and streamline through smarter pricing, leaner staffing models, tighter cost control, and stronger community engagement via loyalty programs and social media," Ms Butler said.

" In many cases, the challenges of COVID and the current cost-of-living crisis have actually strengthened the good operators. "

Kyla and Jason Romaior are planning to move to their Hervey Range property and raise a family. (ABC News: Emily Anderson)

Meanwhile, the Romaiors plan to move in and start a family, opening the place to their friends and family.

They also plan to put in zip lines and swimming holes so their future kids get to have a lot of fun.

"It was less the sentimentality of our wedding and more the excitement of the place and how we can build it for community," Mr Romaior said.

"We are going to move here. This will be our home and our business.

"We're excited for our kids to grow up on property.

"Our relationship is stronger because of it."

title: Wall Street drops for a fifth straight week amid rising US-Iran tensions
text:
US stocks have deepened their drops as Wall Street finished off a fifth straight losing week, its longest such streak in nearly four years.

The S&P 500 fell 1.7 per cent to close its worst week since the war with Iran began. The Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 793 points, or 1.7 per cent, and fell more than 10 per cent from its record set last month, while the Nasdaq composite sank 2.1 per cent.

The losses were a break from Wall Street's pattern this week, where the US stock market flip-flopped from gains to losses each day as hopes rose and fell about a possible end to the war.

Trump looks increasingly trapped Donald Trump appears to be fast running out of exits on his Iran war folly.

Moments after the US stock market finished trading on Thursday, President Donald Trump offered more potential for optimism.

He extended a self-imposed deadline to "obliterate" Iran's power plants to April 6 if it doesn't fully allow oil tankers to exit the Persian Gulf through the Strait of Hormuz to the open ocean.

Oil prices eased immediately afterwards in a sign of hope that some normalcy may return to the strait.

It was similar to the relief that swept markets Monday, when oil prices slid 10 per cent after Trump announced the first delay to his deadline for clearing the Strait of Hormuz.

But oil prices resumed their climb as trading moved westward on Friday from Asia to Europe and back to Wall Street.

US President Donald Trump. (Reuters: Marco Bello)

Despite Trump's latest announcement, fighting continued in the Middle East. Iran gave no signs of backing down, and Israel threatened to "escalate and expand" its attacks on Iran.

"The diplomatic dissonance this week between the US and Iran dismayed investors," said Doug Beath, global equity strategist at Wells Fargo Investment Institute.

"By the end of the week, risk appetite could not withstand the fog of war."

"Any further statements by Trump about a deal are white noise to the markets," Jim Bianco, president and macro strategist at Bianco Research, wrote in a social media post.

" Only if the IRANIANS say the talks are going well will it impact markets. "

The price for a barrel of Brent crude oil climbed 3.4 per cent to settle at US$105.32. That's up from roughly US$70 just before the war began. Benchmark US crude rose 5.5 per cent to settle at US$99.64 per barrel.

The fear in financial markets is that the war will disrupt the Persian Gulf's energy industry for a long time.

That could keep enough oil and natural gas out of the world's markets to send a punishing wave of inflation through the global economy.

Not only would it raise prices for drivers buying gasoline, it could push businesses that use any trucks, ships or planes to move their products to raise their own prices.

It would also make electricity from gas-fired power plants more expensive.

If the war continues until the end of June, strategists at Macquarie say the price of oil could reach US$200 per barrel.

The miscalculation the US and Israel made over Iran Donald Trump is deploying thousands of marines and extra battleships to the Middle East. So is he set to wind down his war with Iran, or is he thinking of escalating?

The record is just above US$147, set during the summer of 2008. That's when Iran's testing of missiles, including one that could reach Israel, and strong demand for oil from China, helped send prices spiking despite the Great Recession.

High gasoline prices and the war are already hitting confidence among US consumers, whose spending makes up the bulk of the economy.

Sentiment among them fell slightly more in March from February than economists expected, according to a survey by the University of Michigan.

On Wall Street, most stocks fell, including three out of every four in the S&P 500. The index, which is the main measure of the US stock market's health, is 8.7 per cent below its all-time high set in January.

Big Tech stocks were among the heaviest weights on the market, including drops of 4 per cent for Amazon, 4 per cent for Meta Platforms and 2.2 per cent for Nvidia.

Companies selling things that are not essentials, which customers could stop buying if they're spending much more on gasoline, also sank sharply.

Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings lost 6.9 per cent, Starbucks dropped 4.8 per cent and Chipotle Mexican Grill sank 4.1 per cent.

All told, the S&P 500 fell 108.31 points to 6,368.85. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 793.47 to 45,166.64, and the Nasdaq composite sank 459.72 to 20,948.36.

The Dow and Nasdaq are both down more than 10 per cent from their records, a steep-enough drop that professional investors have a name for it: a "correction".

In stock markets abroad, indexes fell in Europe following a mixed finish in Asia.

In the bond market, which has helped influence Trump's actions in the past, Treasury yields swivelled.

The yield for the 10-year Treasury rose as high as 4.48 per cent before pulling back to 4.43 per cent.

That's up from 4.42 per cent late Thursday and from just 3.97 per cent before the war began. The rise has already sent rates jumping for mortgages and for other loans taken by US households and businesses, slowing the economy.

High Treasury yields and disruption in the bond market were big factors that Trump named a year ago when he backed off his initial threats for global tariffs made on "Liberation Day".

The moves caused critics to allege Trump always chickens out, or "TACO," if financial markets show enough pain.

AP

title: Foran named interim coach after Manly sack Seibold following 0-3 start
text:
Club legend Kieran Foran has been appointed Manly's interim coach after the Sea Eagles sacked Anthony Seibold only three games into the 2026 NRL season.

The morning after Manly fired fourth-year head coach Seibold, Foran was called to Brookvale Oval where he was appointed to his first head-coaching role.

Manly's last premiership-winning five-eighth, Foran played 196 games for the club across two stints and joined Seibold's coaching staff this year following his retirement.

Manly sacks coach Seibold Manly has sacked head coach Anthony Seibold after a dismal start to his fourth NRL season at the helm.

Foran also works as an analyst for Fox Sports, and said during coverage on Friday night that he was shocked to have heard news of Seibold's axing following Thursday's loss to the Sydney Roosters.

But the 35-year-old will need to compose himself swiftly, as he has been tasked with restoring order at a Manly club in a race to avoid its first wooden spoon.

Sea Eagles chairman Scott Penn said Foran was the right man to lead the club in the immediate future.

"Kieran has given so much to this club over many years and we are delighted that he has accepted this new role," Penn said.

"The fact he has only recently finished his playing career is an advantage, he understands the current pace of the game and what we need to do to compete.

"He is Manly through and through and we know that he will pour all his energy into this new role."

Foran's first game in charge will be Thursday night's round-five clash against the Dolphins.

Seibold yesterday became just the third coach in the NRL era to be shown the door within the first month of a season, after the Sea Eagles' winless start at home.

Brad Arthur, Matt Ballin and Michael Ennis loom as early contenders to replace him on a full-time basis from 2027.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

Willie Peters would likely have been a serious option, before he agreed to become the PNG Chiefs' inaugural coach earlier this week.

Ballin is known to have the support of the club's old boys, having won two premierships alongside King and served on Des Hasler's coaching staff.

The former hooker is now a Brisbane assistant, but still has close links to Manly and would be expected to be interested in the role.

Broncos coach Michael Maguire indicated yesterday that the club would be unlikely to stand in the 42-year-old's way if he wanted the job.

Arthur's previous impact at Manly also should not be overlooked.

He left a lasting impression on several Sea Eagles in his one year as Geoff Toovey's assistant in 2013, with his defensive work helping take them to the grand final.

Arthur is easily the most experienced coach on the market after 10 seasons at Parramatta, before helping turn Leeds around in England in the past two years.

A changing of the guard looms at Brookvale, with just nine players locked in beyond the end of next season and Manly now 15 years on from their last premiership.

Highly-touted half Joey Walsh has been left to develop in the NSW Cup, and will become a free agent in November if not re-signed in advance.

He, Simione Laiafi, Onitoni Large and Hugo Hart are viewed as big prospects, with Tolu Koula, Lehi Hopoate and Haumole Olakau'atu all still relatively young.

AAP

title: WA minister wants reforms to local council laws
text:
The bureaucrats assigned to sort out local councils plagued by infighting and dysfunction should be allowed to stay in their roles longer, WA Local Government Minister Hannah Beazley says.

Both the City of Nedlands and the Town of Port Hedland are set to hold extraordinary elections today following public turmoil that saw the resignations of several councillors.

The City of Nedlands council has had its share of problems. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

Ms Beazley appointed commissioners last year to take control of those councils, but their terms end as soon as the new councils are sworn in.

She told the ABC she would have preferred they were able to stay longer, but the current Local Government laws do not allow that.

"The feedback that I had from absolutely everybody in the sector, it was that they thought that I have that ability [to install the commissioners for longer] and they would wish me to enact it if I did," she said.

" So in terms of really uncovering the issues and really dealing with them, a longer period of time would have inevitably, I think, been a more stabilising factor. "

Ms Beazley has flagged reforms to the Local Government Act to provide more flexibility with commissioner appointments when councils are vacated.

Both Nedlands and Port Hedland have their fair share of problems.

Their respective commissioners had uncovered $27 million worth of widespread risks at Nedlands, while Port Hedland has had a history of dysfunction.

Western Australia's Local Government Minister Hannah Beazley. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

Under current laws, an election to fill the vacated councillor positions must be held "as soon as practicable" after commissioners were appointed.

Ms Beazley said she had looked into every possible option to delay both elections to give the commissioners more time, but noted what they achieved in six months was "absolutely incredible".

Monitors will remain in place at each council to oversee the transition from commissioners to new councillors and mayors.

Low turnout likely

Ms Beazley called on ratepayers to participate in Saturday's poll, but said she expected voter turnout to dip even lower than in October's standard local government elections.

Port Hedland is also holding local council elections. (ABC News: Kimberley Putland)

"I think we'll creep up to around that sort of 20 to 21 per cent mark in both Nedlands and Port Hedland," she said on Tuesday.

" I would have liked to have seen the very public issues at both of those localities enliven people to vote — it doesn't seem like that has happened. "

Loading...

Earlier this month, Ms Beazley suggested the incoming Nedlands council speak with its larger neighbours about borrowing resources or amalgamating fully, given the size of its current problems.

But she said she did not think those comments would have had an impact on voter turnout.

Ministerial powers

The minister is seeking other reforms in the sector, including holding four-year full spill elections and compulsory voting.

Port Hedland is also holding local council elections. (ABC News: Kimberley Putland)

"I'm not interested in a power grab at all," Ms Beazley said.

"What I am interested in is having the right tools available for me, and also the inspector to use in the right circumstance, so we can ensure the best outcomes for the community."

Shadow Local Government Minister Kirrilee Warr agreed the act was outdated, but raised concerns about ministerial power.

WA Shadow Local Government Minister Kirrilee Warr. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

" For a minister to have oversight and power in that area — I would like to see … considered detail around how that would be applied in reality. "

WA Local Government Association president Mark Irwin said he understands the reasoning for having commissioners installed for longer, however raised concerns about reform flagged without a timeline.

Voter fatigue

Many ratepayers at both Nedlands and Port Hedland are hoping for a fresh start.

Port Hedland resident Walter Ngaika, who has lived in the area for 20 years, told the ABC the "place needs a fresh outlook" with "fresh faces".

"The place needs something that the community could feel proud of," he said.

Port Hedland's Civic Centre. (ABC News: Kimberley Putland)

Joan Leaver also wants the new council to stop fighting and follow the rules "as they should be doing".

In the Nedlands boundary, resident Joseph Lieberfreund said the problems made a merger with the neighbouring city more appealing.

Nedlands voter Joseph Lieberfreund wants change. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

"I just feel a sort of malaise, I feel a sense of hopelessness in a way," he said.

"To the point where to be honest, we're sort of looking forward to perhaps merging with Claremont."

Ratepayers in both LGAs have until 6pm today to hand deliver postal votes to specific locations.

title: EPA, developer defend records over contentious WA beach development
text:
Western Australia's environmental watchdog and a prominent Perth property developer have defended the sign-off of a contentious tourism development in the state's South West amid growing criticism of the decision.

The Environmental Protection Authority (EPA) recommended the $280 million development at Smiths Beach, near Yallingup and about 265 kilometres south of Perth, for approval last month, despite community concerns.

The project, backed by Perth property developer Adrian Fini, will be subject to several conditions limiting its environmental impact.

The City of Busselton has since lodged a scathing appeal against the project, arguing the EPA had allowed the developer to understate the impact on the natural and visual environment of Smiths Beach the development would have.

"This appeal is based on departures by the EPA from its own objectives and functions, and its failure to have proper regard to the Statement of Environmental Principles," Busselton Chief Executive Garry Hunt wrote in the publicly released letter.

The city has since been joined by the Conservation Council, which offered similar criticism of the watchdog's methods.

Critics have also taken aim at authority members' links to developers, with chair Darren Walsh, deputy chair Jane Bennett and members David Caddy and Hamish Beck all declaring conflicts of interest in the Smiths Beach matter.

"The extent of these conflicts are involvement and knowledge of the assessment in previous employment, and/or professional association with the proponent," an EPA spokesperson said.

The decision leaves the project in the hands of the Appeals Convenor Eve Drain, with Environment Minister Matthew Swinbourn having the final say.

The proposed development includes a hotel, 61 holiday homes and a campground. (Supplied: Smiths Beach Project)

Developer defends 'rigorous work'

In a statement, a spokesman for Fini Group's Smith's Beach Project Team said it had done extensive work to support the project.

"We have provided more technical documentation and analysis completed by experts in the field to support our application than for any other project we have done," the spokesman said.

" We have been open and transparent throughout the process. "

The spokesman said the technical work submitted to support the development had been reviewed and approved by the EPA, and pledged to work with the watchdog to address any ongoing issues.

Matter of public confidence

The vehemence of the City of Busselton's criticism in its letter of appeal has surprised even the project's local opponents.

"The [council letter] was really blistering and scathing, both of the project itself and how the EPA has conducted their assessment," Smiths Beach Action Group spokesman David Mitchell said.

David Mitchell says he was surprised by the vehemence of the council's criticism. (ABC South West WA: Pip Waller)

But the biggest questions came around the confidence the community could have in the watchdog overall.

"We've got an EPA that's directly linked to development, and they're departing from their own principles," Mr Mitchell said.

"What has changed within the EPA between now and 2009 [when the EPA rejected a development in the same area], and what will the minister do?"

The City of Busselton is worried about the environmental impact of the development. (ABC South West: Anthony Pancia)

In addition to Smiths Beach, the Cook government's proposed Burswood racetrack and plans for a ferry terminal at Matilda Bay are among the politically and locally contentious projects either cleared or not assessed by the authority in recent months.

For Vasse MP Libby Mettam, Yallingup's local MP and a former Liberal leader who has held the seat for two decades, the relationships between EPA board members and developers ring alarm bells.

"This smashes public confidence when it comes to the EPA," the Liberal frontbencher said.

"The fact a decision can be made that contradicts the EPA's own independent advice.

Libby Mettam says the decision "smashes" confidence in the EPA. (ABC News: Courtney Withers)

" What is currently being proposed ... ignores state planning policy, previous rulings and independent peer review. "

Environmental campaigner turned consultant Piers Verstegen said the EPA's problems were clear.

"It's not just environmental groups raising the usual issues, but across the community there seems to be a level of concern I have not seen before, even under previous governments," he said.

Piers Verstegen says there is a growing sense of concern about the EPA's approach. (ABC News: Elicia Kennedy)

Watchdog defends record

In a statement to the ABC, the EPA said its processes to manage conflicts of interest were working.

"The EPA exercises a high degree of care and caution when it considers potential conflicts of interest," a spokesperson said.

"Seeking independent probity advice on potential and perceived conflicts is also a standard part of the EPA's conflict of interest procedures.

"Four EPA members, including the chair, declared conflicts of interest in the Smiths Beach proposal and were not involved in this assessment."

The EPA says it recognised and addressed community concerns over development in the area. (ABC News: Gian De Poloni)

The spokesperson said it had recognised and addressed the community concerns over Smiths Beach.

"It was this strong community connection that led EPA members to visit the site and meet members of the Smiths Beach Action Group to hear concerns," a spokesperson said.

"During this same visit, the EPA also met City of Busselton staff to gain further insight into their key issues."

Mr Swinbourn declined to comment, citing the ongoing appeals process.
